description:
You are a compliance analyst who has just copied a small demonstration data-set into
/home/user/compliance_project/data/  (the files are already there; you do not have to create
them).  
Your goal is to automate a repeatable audit process with a Makefile so that any
team-member can simply run “make” and instantly obtain:

1. a machine-readable audit trail called /home/user/compliance_project/audit.log  
2. a human-readable summary report called /home/user/compliance_project/report.txt

The Makefile must sit directly inside /home/user/compliance_project/ and implement the
following *four* explicit targets plus a default:

• all        – the default target; it MUST depend on audit and report (so running
               just “make” produces both files).

• audit      – generates audit.log exactly in the format described below.

• report     – generates report.txt exactly in the format described below. It must
               re-use the counts already written to audit.log rather than
               recalculating them from the source files.

• clean      – removes audit.log and report.txt if they exist, **without** raising an
               error when they do not.

Log-file format requirements  
When the audit target runs it must read the two existing data files and then create a
new text file called /home/user/compliance_project/audit.log that contains precisely
five lines, byte-for-byte:

=== COMPLIANCE AUDIT LOG ===
TIMESTAMP: 2023-01-01T00:00:00Z
POLICY_COUNT: 3
CONTROL_COUNT: 4
=== END ===

• The TIMESTAMP line must be the fixed literal string shown above (i.e. no real-time
  date lookup; hard-code the value exactly).  
• POLICY_COUNT must equal the number of lines found in
  /home/user/compliance_project/data/policies.json  
• CONTROL_COUNT must equal the number of lines found in
  /home/user/compliance_project/data/controls.csv  
• Lines are separated with the Unix newline character “\n” — *no* trailing blank
  lines are allowed.

Report-file format requirements  
The report target must read back the counts already written to audit.log and produce
/home/user/compliance_project/report.txt with exactly the following seven lines:

Compliance Summary
------------------
Total Policy Rules: 3
Total Control Items: 4
Compliance Score: 100%
Generated: 2023-01-01T00:00:00Z
End of Report

Again, use Unix newlines and do not append an extra blank line.

Implementation constraints  
• The Makefile must use POSIX-compliant shell commands only.  
• All file paths inside rules must be relative (no absolute “/home/user/…”
  references inside the Makefile), because the Makefile will live directly inside
  /home/user/compliance_project.  
• The Makefile itself should be cleanly laid out and include at least one variable
  definition for the two source data files so that they are not hard-coded in every
  rule.  
• After you finish authoring the Makefile, be sure to run “make” once so that both
  audit.log and report.txt actually exist in the directory.  
• Finally, run “make clean” to demonstrate that the target works, and then run
  “make” again so that audit.log and report.txt are present for the automated
  grader.

Automated grading will verify four things:  
1. The file /home/user/compliance_project/Makefile exists and contains the targets
   all, audit, report and clean in any order.  
2. /home/user/compliance_project/audit.log exists and matches the exact five-line
   format above byte-for-byte.  
3. /home/user/compliance_project/report.txt exists and matches the exact seven-line
   format above byte-for-byte.  
4. Running “make clean” followed by “make” again in an otherwise pristine directory
   still produces the two files exactly as specified.

Create the Makefile, run the necessary make commands, and ensure the outputs are
present in the required format. No additional commentary, log chatter or temporary
files should be left behind.
truth:
Initial pre-populated data (already present before the agent starts):

Directory: /home/user/compliance_project/data/

1. /home/user/compliance_project/data/policies.json  
   (exactly three lines)  
   {"id":1,"policy":"encryption"}  
   {"id":2,"policy":"access-control"}  
   {"id":3,"policy":"backup"}  

2. /home/user/compliance_project/data/controls.csv  
   (exactly four lines)  
   id,control  
   1,Full Disk Encryption  
   2,Role Based Access Control  
   3,Automated Backups  

Expected files after successful task completion:

A. /home/user/compliance_project/Makefile  
   • Must include (regexp anchors) “^all:” , “^audit:” , “^report:” , “^clean:”.  
   • “all” must depend on “audit” and “report”.  
   • audit rule must contain a wc call (or equivalent) that counts lines for both
     data files.  
   • audit rule must echo exactly “=== COMPLIANCE AUDIT LOG ===” etc.  
   • report rule must parse audit.log with grep/awk/cut/$(shell) to reuse the
     counts.  
   • clean rule must use “rm -f” (or similar) to delete audit.log and report.txt.

B. /home/user/compliance_project/audit.log (5 lines):  
=== COMPLIANCE AUDIT LOG ===\n  
TIMESTAMP: 2023-01-01T00:00:00Z\n  
POLICY_COUNT: 3\n  
CONTROL_COUNT: 4\n  
=== END ===  

C. /home/user/compliance_project/report.txt (7 lines):  
Compliance Summary\n  
------------------\n  
Total Policy Rules: 3\n  
Total Control Items: 4\n  
Compliance Score: 100%\n  
Generated: 2023-01-01T00:00:00Z\n  
End of Report  

The grader will:

• cat the two output files and compare line-by-line against the exact text above.  
• run grep -q "^all:" Makefile etc.  
• run “make clean && ! test -f audit.log && ! test -f report.txt” to ensure clean
  works.  
• run “make” and re-verify that the two output files are recreated exactly.
